title: Floor 4→6 boss encounter is 3.36x harder than preceding floor (all profiles)
description: Across all 6 skill profiles and 28,000 runs, the average turns per encounter jumps from 6.68 on floor 4 to 22.44 on floor 6 — a 3.36x ratio that exceeds the 2.0x spike threshold by 68%. Floor 6 hosts two boss-tier enemies: The Final Exam (27.5 avg turns, 77-97% win rate depending on profile) and The Burning Deadline (23.6 avg turns, 77% win rate for new_player). For weaker profiles the damage spike compounds: language_learner loses 30.7 avg HP on floor 6 encounters vs 6-10 HP on floors 1-4. This is the single largest difficulty cliff in the game.

evidence.metric: Floor 4 avg turns: 6.68 | Floor 6 avg turns: 22.44 | ratio 3.36x (threshold: 2.0x). Floor 6 death rate: 7.8% vs floor 4: 1.6%. n=7,465 floor-6 encounters across 28,000 runs

reproductionSteps: Read analytics/enemy-analysis.md, compare floor 4 vs floor 6 avg turns and death rate
suggestedFix: Reduce floor 6 boss HP or damage by 20-30%, or add a difficulty bridge encounter on floor 5 to smooth the ramp. Alternatively reduce The Burning Deadline's damage output for lower mastery levels.

title: Floor 10→12 encounter spike (2.06x turns) hits low-skill profiles disproportionately
description: Floor 12 encounters average 15.69 turns vs floor 10's 7.62 — a 2.06x jump that crosses the structural 2x threshold. Disaggregated by profile it is worse: new_player sees floor 10→12 go from 11.4→22.8 turns (2.0x), master@asc20 sees 9.4→23.2 turns (2.47x). The Curriculum on floor 12 has a 37% spike rate (>50% of HP taken in one fight) for new_player. This spike coincides with the act-2 boss tier and is structurally separate from the floor-6 spike.

evidence.metric: Floor 10 avg turns: 7.62 | Floor 12 avg turns: 15.69 | ratio 2.06x. The Curriculum (floor 12): 62% win rate, 37% damage spike rate for new_player (n=193). master@asc20 floor 10→12: 9.4→23.2 turns (2.47x)

reproductionSteps: In per-run JSON, aggregate encounters by floor and compute avg turns floor 10 vs 12
suggestedFix: Tune The Curriculum's HP or attack pattern for act 2 entry; add a floor 11 mid-tier encounter to bridge the gap.

title: Floor 18 final bosses (The Omnibus / The Final Lesson) delete 87-91% of encounters via 50%+ HP damage
description: The Omnibus and The Final Lesson on floor 18 are catastrophically damaging: 91% and 87% of encounters against these enemies respectively result in the player losing more than 50% of their starting HP. The Omnibus averages 79.5 HP taken (out of 100 starting) per encounter for new_player, with a 67% defeat rate. Average turns are 23.6-24.7 — these are slow, grinding fights that end in near-certain death for players below median skill. The floor 18 death rate across all runs is 30.2% — higher than any other floor by 6x.

evidence.metric: Omnibus: 91% spike rate, 79.5 avg HP taken, 67% defeat rate for new_player (n=323). Final Lesson: 87% spike rate, 71.4 avg HP taken, 64% defeat rate (n=278). Floor 18 death rate: 30.2% vs next worst floor 6 at 7.8%

reproductionSteps: In per-run JSON, filter encounters floor==18 and compute damageTaken/playerHpBefore ratio
suggestedFix: Reduce The Omnibus and The Final Lesson base damage by 25-30%. Consider adding a rest room before floor 18 to allow healing. The current 30.2% floor-18 death rate is the primary funnel failure point for a full run.

title: Ascension 20 is unplayable: experienced@asc20 wins 2.7%, avg death floor 3
description: The experienced profile at ascension 20 achieves a 2.7% win rate with an average of 4.0 floors reached. This is far below the too_hard threshold (target_win×0.5 = 32.5%) and the avg_floors<5 criterion escalates severity to HIGH. Of 1,000 runs, 285 (28.5%) die on floor 1 alone. This pattern persists for master@asc20 at 9.1% (still below the 32.5% threshold). Ascension 20 as implemented provides no viable path to completion — it functions as a wall, not a challenge.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc20: win=2.7%, avg_floors=4.0, floor-1 deaths=285/1000 (28.5%). master@asc20: win=9.1%, avg_floors=8.3. too_hard_threshold=32.5% (target 65% × 0.5)

reproductionSteps: Check survival rate and deathFloor distribution
suggestedFix: Audit ascension 20 modifiers — current scaling produces a run that cannot be completed by even experienced players (76% accuracy, high charge rate). Target win rate should be 10-20% for max ascension. Consider capping enemy HP scaling multiplier at asc15 and applying flat attack increases instead.

title: experienced profile at ascension 0 wins 94.5% — above too_easy ceiling
description: The experienced profile (proxy for a dedicated returning player at 76% accuracy, high charge rate) wins 94.5% of runs at ascension 0. The too_easy threshold is target_win×1.3 = 84.5%. This is 10 percentage points above the ceiling, meaning experienced players have essentially no challenge at base difficulty. The win rate variance (std_dev=0.228) is healthy but the absolute level removes tension from the majority of runs.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc0: win=94.5% (n=4000). too_easy threshold: 65%×1.3=84.5%. Variance std_dev=0.228 (healthy). Avg floors: 17.8/18

suggestedFix: Encourage experienced players to play ascension 3-5 as a 'normal challenge' baseline. Consider a soft prompt after 3 wins at asc0 suggesting higher ascension.

title: New player encounters average 13.8 turns — nearly double the 8-turn engagement ceiling
description: The new_player profile takes an average of 13.8 turns per encounter (14,693 encounters analyzed). The engagement_tedious_grind threshold is 8 turns. At 13.8 turns, fights feel like slow attrition — particularly for a new player who is still learning the system. Language_learner is at 12.9 turns. This is driven by low mastery (cards deal 3-8 damage at L0) and the inability to charge (50% accuracy), meaning every fight takes 2-3x as many attacks to kill enemies whose HP scales for mid-game mastery levels.

evidence.metric: new_player: avg 13.8 turns/enc (n=14,693). language_learner: avg 12.9 turns/enc (n=11,932). Tedious threshold: >8 turns. Master@asc20 also hits 13.0 turns/enc

suggestedFix: Raise base card damage at mastery level 0 for strike (3→5) and ensure enemy HP at floor 1 allows sub-10 turn victories without charging. New player fights should feel resolvable, not like grinding.

title: language_learner profile HP drains to 37.4 average after floor 5 — below the 40 HP healing floor
description: The language_learner profile (35% accuracy, 61% charge rate) reaches an average HP of 37.4 after encounters past floor 5 — below the balance_healing_insufficient threshold of PLAYER_HP_START×0.4 = 40. This indicates the game's healing economy (rest rooms, healing cards) is insufficient to offset the incoming damage from later floors for players with low quiz accuracy. This profile also sees 16.6% of encounters take >50% of starting HP.

evidence.metric: language_learner: avg HP after floor 5+ encounters = 37.4 (threshold: 40). Damage spike rate: 16.6% of 11,932 encounters. Win rate: 19.8%

reproductionSteps: Aggregate playerHpAfter for encounters with floor >= 5
suggestedFix: Increase healing at rest rooms for players below 50% HP. Consider giving language/vocab-focused runs a 'catch-up healing' relic that provides bonus HP recovery when accuracy is low.

title: language_learner wrong-answer rate is 64.9% — above the 60% frustration ceiling
description: The language_learner profile answers 64.9% of card plays incorrectly (265,231 wrong out of 408,824 plays). The engagement_frustrating_streaks threshold is 60% wrong/played for new-player-tier profiles. At nearly 2/3 wrong answers, the game loop for language learners is predominantly: attempt charge → get it wrong → fizzle. The 0.50x fizzle ratio is protective, but sustained 65% wrong-answer rates mean the player is more often punished than rewarded.

evidence.metric: language_learner: wrong=265,231 / played=408,824 = 64.9%. Threshold: >60%. This profile has 35% accuracy and 61% charge rate meaning most charge attempts fail

reproductionSteps: Sum totalWrong and totalCardsPlayed across all runs, compute ratio
suggestedFix: Consider reducing the charge-attempt rate for language learners who have low historical accuracy — let the bot (and real players) lean on Quick Play more. Alternatively provide a tooltip or UI hint after 5+ consecutive wrong answers suggesting Quick Play.

title: master@asc20 timeout rate reaches 5.43% — crossing the 5% structural threshold
description: The master@asc20 profile (85% accuracy, high mastery) sees 5.43% of encounters end in timeout — just above the 5% structural threshold. This is notable because it occurs in the highest-skill profile under the hardest ascension, suggesting enemy HP values at asc20 can exceed the player's damage output even with near-perfect play. Combined with the 9.1% win rate, timeout-induced stalemates are a real second failure mode at max ascension.

evidence.metric: master@asc20: 433 timeouts / 7,973 encounters = 5.43%. Threshold: >5%. master@asc15: 1.6% (within normal range). language_learner: 2.68%

suggestedFix: Cap enemy HP at asc20 to ensure even low-damage runs can technically win within the turn limit. Check if the timeout limit scales with enemy HP or stays fixed.

title: language_learner 16.6% and competent 13.5% of encounters take >50% HP in a single fight
description: Both the language_learner (16.6% of 11,932 encounters) and competent (13.5% of 13,589 encounters) profiles exceed the 10% damage spike threshold — meaning more than 1 in 8 fights drains over half of starting HP. For competent this spikes further: The Burning Deadline on floor 6 averages 30.7 HP taken vs 89 HP start (34.5% ratio per encounter, but 24% of individual competent fights go above 50% HP).

evidence.metric: language_learner: 16.6% spike rate (1,981/11,932 encounters). competent: 13.5% spike rate (1,834/13,589 encounters). Balance threshold: >10%

suggestedFix: Reduce The Burning Deadline damage scaling for players with mastery < 2. Consider a damage reduction mechanic for players at low HP entering floor 6 boss fights.

title: Ascension 15-20 win rates indicate a progression cliff where difficulty outpaces player agency
description: The engagement_low_fun rule fires when regular/gamer proxies fall below too_hard_threshold. At ascension 15, experienced@asc15 drops to 55% (within range), but master@asc15 is 31% — only marginally above the 32.5% too_hard threshold. At asc20 both profiles are catastrophically below: experienced@asc20 at 2.7% and master@asc20 at 9.1%. The progression from asc10 (72%/42%) to asc15 (55%/31%) to asc20 (2.7%/9.1%) shows a non-linear cliff rather than a smooth curve — asc20 isn't 'harder', it's a different game.

evidence.metric: experienced@asc10: 72%, asc15: 55%, asc20: 2.7%. master@asc10: 42%, asc15: 31%, asc20: 9.1%. The drop from asc15→asc20 is 52pp for experienced (vs 17pp for asc10→asc15)

reproductionSteps: Compare win rates across ascension levels
suggestedFix: The asc15→asc20 gap needs a difficulty audit. Either add ascension levels 16-19 as stepping stones, or tune asc20 so that it targets 15-25% win rate rather than 3-9%. A player should be able to see what they're working toward.

title: volatile_core relic has 6% observational win rate vs 59% baseline — power score 0.378
description: The volatile_core relic appears in 250 runs and achieves only 6.0% win rate vs 59.6% for non-holders. The survivorship-free relic-performance report confirms the signal: 7.0 floors after acquisition (vs 17.0 for top relics), power score 0.378 vs 0.680-0.700 cluster for all other relics. Per cross-checking individual profiles: volatile_core holders in experienced profile win 42.9% (n=7) vs 94.7% for non-holders; master holders win 20% (n=5) vs 71.3%. The relic is either mechanically broken (its effect may not trigger as intended) or so punishing that acquiring it is a net negative.

evidence.metric: volatile_core: 250 appearances, 6.0% win rate (-53.6% delta vs baseline). Power score 0.378 vs cluster mean ~0.67. 7.0 avg floors after acquisition. Experienced: 42.9% win with vs 94.7% without (n=7 vs 1993)

reproductionSteps: In relic-performance.md and relic-analysis.md, compare volatile_core vs baseline. Check src/data/relics/ for volatile_core definition.
suggestedFix: Audit volatile_core's implementation. The -53.6% observational delta is the largest negative relic signal in the dataset by far. Either the relic description doesn't match its effect, or it creates an unwinnable game state. Check if it permanently modifies HP/damage in a way that stacks negatively.

title: build_strength archetype at 41% win rate — 2.1x gap below build_poison (87%)
description: The 8 tested archetypes span a 2.1x win rate gap from build_strength (41%) to build_poison (87%). build_fortress at 84% and build_poison at 87% are both above the too_easy threshold for a 65%-target profile. build_strength at 41% and build_chain/build_tempo at 54% indicate these archetypes are undertuned relative to their counterparts. All archetypes share nearly identical deck compositions (42-48% attack, 46-50% shield) so the gap comes entirely from mechanic choices — strength-scaling mechanics underperform poison/fortress mechanics.

evidence.metric: build_poison: 87%, build_fortress: 84%, build_control: 73%, build_exhaust: 65%, build_chain/tempo: 54%, build_strength: 41%. HP efficiency delta: fortress=34.66x vs strength=12.62x. build_strength avg mastery 2.5 vs poison 4.0

reproductionSteps: Compare archetype-analysis.md win rates and HP efficiency
suggestedFix: Buff strength-scaling mechanics (reckless, heavy_strike, bash) or adjust the bot profile's card selection logic to better support the strength archetype's win condition. Flag for balance-sim follow-up.

title: eruption card carries -14.4% win-rate delta with 3,741 affected runs — potential archetype trap
description: The eruption card appears in 3,741 runs (via card-analysis) with a correlated win rate of 45% vs the 59% baseline — a -14.4% delta. This is the largest negative card-level win signal in the dataset. With 125,486 total plays and 2.22 average play frequency per run, eruption is a common card that correlates strongly with losing. This may indicate it is a 'trap' card that players draft because it appears useful (18.4 avg dmg) but underperforms in actual play contexts. The barbed_edge PRE-EXISTING-1 bug could be inflating eruption's effective damage in playtests while the real game is lower.

evidence.metric: eruption: 125,486 plays, 3,741 runs, win%=45% vs 59% baseline, delta=-14.4%. Avg dmg/play=18.4, floorDelta=+0.2 (near neutral on floors, but win-rate-negative)

reproductionSteps: In card-analysis.md, sort by delta and check eruption row
suggestedFix: Investigate whether eruption is being drawn too frequently in the starting deck, causing players to over-invest in the mechanic. Cross-reference with barbed_edge bug resolution — after fixing PRE-EXISTING-1, eruption's real damage output may be even lower.

title: execute card actual charge rate is 9% per encounter data vs 24% in card-analysis — possible tracking discrepancy
description: The card-analysis report shows execute with a 24% charge rate. Manual extraction from per-encounter cardPlay arrays shows only 9.0% of execute plays are charged (136/1,515 plays across 4 profiles). This 2.7x discrepancy may indicate the analytics aggregation weights charge attempts differently from actual charges, or execute has a conditional charging mechanic (enemy HP threshold) that the aggregate obscures. This warrants investigation of analytics computation methodology.

evidence.metric: card-analysis.md: execute charge%=24% (2,322 plays). Per-encounter cardPlay extraction: execute charged=136/1,515 plays = 9.0%. Discrepancy factor: 2.7x

reproductionSteps: In card-analysis.md row for execute compare charge% vs manual count from encounter cardPlays
suggestedFix: Verify analytics-report.ts charge rate aggregation logic for cards with conditional charging. Execute may only charge when enemy is below HP threshold — this would suppress charge opportunities and be expected behavior, but should be documented.
